content.text:
When we studied triangles, we discovered that the sum of interior 
angles is always $180°$—a fundamental truth that underlies all 
of Euclidean geometry. But what about figures with more sides? 
Quadrilaterals have four angles, pentagons have five, hexagons 
have six. Is there a pattern that governs these angle sums?

The answer reveals one of geometry's most elegant relationships: 
every polygon's angle sum connects back to the triangle. By 
dividing any polygon into triangles, we can unlock the secret 
of its angles. This triangulation principle appears throughout 
mathematics and has practical applications in architecture, 
engineering, and computer graphics.
Polygons and Their Names

content.title: Polygon
content.content:
A **polygon** is a closed plane figure formed 
by three or more line segments called **sides**, 
where:

- Each side intersects exactly two other sides

- The intersections occur only at endpoints (called **vertices**)

- No two sides with a common vertex are collinear

content.text:
The word "polygon" comes from the Greek *poly* (many) and 
*gonia* (angle). Thus a polygon is literally a "many-angled" 
figure. We name polygons by their number of sides, using Greek 
prefixes:
Convex and Concave Polygons

content.title: Convex and Concave Polygons
content.content:
A polygon is **convex** if no diagonal 
lies outside the polygon—equivalently, if all interior angles 
are less than $180°$.

A polygon is **concave** (or 
**non-convex**) if at least one diagonal lies outside the 
polygon, which means at least one interior angle exceeds $180°$.

content.text:
An intuitive way to think about convexity: if you stand inside a 
convex polygon, you can see every part of every wall. In a concave 
polygon, parts of some walls are hidden from certain viewpoints.
Interior Angle Sum

How can we find the sum of interior angles for any polygon? The key 
insight is *triangulation*: we can divide any convex polygon 
into non-overlapping triangles by drawing diagonals from a single 
vertex.

Notice the pattern: a polygon with $n$ sides can be divided into 
exactly $(n-2)$ triangles. Since each triangle contributes $180°$ 
to the total, we get:

content.title: Polygon Interior Angle Sum
content.content:
The sum of the interior angles of a convex polygon with $n$ sides is:
\[
 S = (n - 2) \cdot 180°
\]
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
From any vertex of a convex $n$-gon, we can draw diagonals to all 
non-adjacent vertices. This creates $(n-2)$ non-overlapping triangles. 
The angles of all these triangles together form the interior angles 
of the polygon. Since each triangle has angle sum $180°$, the total is:
\[
 (n-2) \cdot 180°
\]

content.title: Finding angle sums
content.problem:
Find the sum of interior angles of:

1. A pentagon

2. An octagon

3. A 20-gon

content.title: Finding number of sides from angle sum
content.problem:
A polygon has an interior angle sum of $1800°$. How many sides does 
it have?
content.solution:
We solve $(n-2) \cdot 180° = 1800°$:
$$
 (n-2) \cdot 180 &= 1800 \\
 n - 2 &= 10 \\
 n &= 12
$$
The polygon has 12 sides (a dodecagon).

content.title: Finding a missing angle
content.problem:
In pentagon $ABCDE$, angles $A$, $B$, $C$, and $D$ measure $108°$, 
$92°$, $135°$, and $87°$ respectively. Find $m\angle E$.
content.solution:
The sum of interior angles in a pentagon is $(5-2) \cdot 180° = 540°$.

Therefore:
$$
 108° + 92° + 135° + 87° + m\angle E &= 540° \\
 422° + m\angle E &= 540° \\
 m\angle E &= 118°
$$

content.text: Regular Polygons

content.title: Regular Polygon
content.content:
A **regular polygon** is a polygon in 
which:

- All sides are congruent (equilateral)

- All angles are congruent (equiangular)

content.text:
Regular polygons possess the highest degree of symmetry among all 
polygons with the same number of sides. The ancient Greeks were 
fascinated by regular polygons, seeing in their perfect symmetry 
a reflection of divine order.

Since all angles of a regular $n$-gon are congruent, we can find 
each interior angle by dividing the total by $n$:

content.title: Interior Angle of a Regular Polygon
content.content:
Each interior angle of a regular $n$-gon measures:
\[
 \frac{(n-2) \cdot 180°}{n}
\]
content.label: Theorem

content.title: Regular polygon angles
content.problem:
Find each interior angle of:

1. A regular hexagon

2. A regular decagon

content.title: Finding sides from interior angle
content.problem:
Each interior angle of a regular polygon measures $150°$. How many 
sides does it have?
content.solution:
Using the formula $\displaystyle\frac{(n-2) \cdot 180°}{n} = 150°$:
$$
 (n-2) \cdot 180 &= 150n \\
 180n - 360 &= 150n \\
 30n &= 360 \\
 n &= 12
$$
The polygon has 12 sides (a regular dodecagon).

content.text: Exterior Angles

At each vertex of a polygon, an **exterior angle** 
is formed by one side and the extension of the adjacent side.

Notice that at each vertex, the interior angle and exterior angle 
are supplementary (they form a linear pair and sum to $180°$).

content.title: Exterior Angle Sum
content.content:
The sum of the exterior angles of any convex polygon (one at each 
vertex) is always:
\[
 360°
\]
content.label: Theorem

content.text:
This remarkable theorem says that no matter how many sides a polygon 
has—whether three or three hundred—its exterior angles always 
sum to exactly $360°$, one full rotation.

content.content:
At each vertex, the interior and exterior angles are supplementary. 
For an $n$-gon, the sum of all interior and exterior angle pairs is:
\[
 n \cdot 180°
\]
The sum of interior angles is $(n-2) \cdot 180°$. Therefore, the 
sum of exterior angles is:
$$
 n \cdot 180° - (n-2) \cdot 180° &= 180°[n - (n-2)] \\
 &= 180° \cdot 2 \\
 &= 360°
$$

content.title: Exterior angles of regular polygons
content.problem:
Find each exterior angle of:

1. A regular hexagon

2. A regular 15-gon
content.solution:
Since all exterior angles of a regular polygon are congruent, each 
measures $\displaystyle\frac{360°}{n}$:

1. Hexagon: $\displaystyle\frac{360°}{6} = 60°$

2. 15-gon: $\displaystyle\frac{360°}{15} = 24°$

content.title: Using exterior angles to find sides
content.problem:
Each exterior angle of a regular polygon measures $20°$. How many 
sides does it have?
content.solution:
Since exterior angles sum to $360°$:
\[
 n = \frac{360°}{20°} = 18
\]
The polygon has 18 sides.

content.text: Regular Tessellations

A **tessellation** (or **tiling**) is 
an arrangement of polygons that covers a plane with no gaps or 
overlaps. When can regular polygons tessellate?

For regular polygons to fit around a point without gaps, their 
interior angles must divide evenly into $360°$. This only works for:

These are the only three regular polygons that tessellate the plane 
by themselves. The hexagonal tessellation appears in nature in 
honeycombs—bees instinctively use the most efficient shape for 
storing honey while using minimal wax.

marginNotes.text:
There is a time for everything, 
and a season for every activity under heaven.

Let your eyes look straight ahead; 
fix your gaze directly before you.

For from him and through him and 
for him are all things.

The heavens declare the glory of God; 
the skies proclaim the work of his hands.

He stretches out the heavens like a 
canopy, and spreads them out like a tent to live in.

God saw all that he had made, and it 
was very good.

marginNotes.author: Euclid
marginNotes.text: The whole is greater than the part.

marginNotes.author: Proclus
marginNotes.text: The whole becomes known through its parts.

marginNotes.author: M.C. Escher
marginNotes.text: We adore chaos because we love to produce order.
